User Story: As a CLI user, I want running benchmark with no arguments to show help so that I can discover commands.
Expected Behavior: When sys.argv has only the program name, main() calls parser.print_help() and returns exit code 0 without running analysis.
Test Result: Exit 0; printed full argparse usage with share/rate/config subcommands and no analysis run.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want --version to print version info so that I can confirm the installed build.
Expected Behavior: Top-level --version prints: 'Privacy-Compliant Peer Benchmark Tool', 'Version: 3.0.0', Python version, and platform; returns exit code 0.
Test Result: Exit 0; printed tool name, Version 3.0.0, Python 3.12.3, Linux platform.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want help text to list available presets so that I can choose one without a separate command.
Expected Behavior: create_parser() epilog includes EXAMPLES and get_presets_help() output listing preset names and descriptions when PresetManager loads successfully.
Test Result: Exit 0; --help epilog includes EXAMPLES and AVAILABLE PRESETS listing all 6 shipped presets with descriptions.
Notes: UX: Help EXAMPLES reference obsolete preset names (standard, conservative) not in PresetManager choices.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want config list to enumerate presets so that I can see available configurations.
Expected Behavior: benchmark config list prints preset_mgr.format_preset_list() (names, descriptions, compliance posture, key optimization settings) and returns exit code 0.
Test Result: Exit 0; listed 6 presets with descriptions, compliance posture, max weight, and tolerance.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want config show PRESET to display full preset YAML so that I can inspect settings.
Expected Behavior: benchmark config show <preset_name> prints format_preset_detail() including version, description, compliance_posture, full YAML, and usage example; returns 0. If preset missing, prints not-found message listing available presets.
Test Result: Exit 0; showed balanced_default version, description, compliance_posture, full YAML, and usage example.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want config validate to check a YAML file so that I know it is schema-valid.
Expected Behavior: On success prints '[OK] Configuration file is valid: <path>' and returns 0. On failure prints '[FAIL] Configuration validation failed:' plus errors and returns 1.
Test Result: Exit 0; printed [OK] Configuration file is valid: config/template.yaml.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want config generate to copy the template so that I can start a custom config.
Expected Behavior: Copies config/template.yaml to output path if template exists and output does not exist; prints '[OK] Configuration template created: <path>' plus edit/validate hints; returns 0. Returns 1 if template missing, output exists, or copy fails.
Test Result: Exit 0; printed [OK] Configuration template created: /tmp/my_benchmark_config.yaml with edit/validate hints.

User Story: As a CLI user, I want running config without a subcommand to show usage so that I know valid config commands.

Test Result: Exit 1; printed Usage: benchmark config {list|show|validate|generate} and subcommand descriptions.

User Story: As an analyst, I want the Privacy Validation sheet/CSV to expose cap, tolerance, constraint detail, and structural margins so that audits are self-contained.
Expected Behavior: to_dataframe emits Compliant, Primary_Cap_Passed, Additional_Constraints_Enforced/Relaxed/Passed, Additional_Constraint_Detail, Structural_Infeasible_Peer/Category, Violation_Margin_%, Strict_Compliant, etc.
Test Result: pytest tests/test_privacy_validation_result.py: 2 passed; emits Additional_Constraint_Detail Structural_Infeasible columns

User Story: As an analyst, I want _TIME_TOTAL_ dimension rows to validate primary caps only so that monthly totals are not subject to per-category secondary tiers.

User Story: As an operator, I want CLI flags for privacy_basis, digital wallet, top merchant, dual axis, and recurring deliverable declarations so that policy gates can be satisfied or triggered explicitly.
